1: dĕvar YHWH ʾasher hayah ʾel Yoʾel ben Pĕtuʾel: “The word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel.” Joel (Yoʾel) = “YHWH is God,” a theophoric name combining the divine names Yo (YHWH) and El. Pethuel is otherwise unknown; some MSS read “Bethuel” (the father of Rebekah, Gen 22:22). No king is mentioned in the superscription — unlike Hosea, Amos, Isaiah, and Micah — making Joel’s date one of the most disputed in the prophetic corpus. Proposals range from pre-exilic (9th century) to post-exilic (5th century BCE); the absence of Assyria and Babylon as named threats and the prominence of the temple cult favor a post-exilic setting for many scholars.
2: shimʿu zoʾt hazzĕqenim wĕhaʾazinu kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets: “Hear this, you elders; listen closely, all inhabitants of the land.” The dual summons formula (shimʿu... haʾazinu) opens the prophetic oracle with two imperatives from different roots: shamaʿ (to hear, attend) and ʾazan (to give ear, often used in poetry with shamaʿ; cf. Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2). Hazzĕqenim (the elders) are the communal leadership; kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets (all inhabitants of the land) includes everyone. The rhetorical question “Has anything like this ever happened in your days, or in the days of your fathers?” positions the locust plague as historically unprecedented — a hyperbole that signals divine rather than merely natural causation.
3: ʿaleha livneikhem sapperu uvneikhem livnehem uvnehem lĕdor ʾacher: “Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children.” The chain of generational transmission (ʾav → ben → ben → dor ʾacher) is the same mechanism commanded for the Passover narrative (Exod 12:26-27; 13:8; Deut 6:20-21). The locust plague is to be memorialized as a foundational event — a theological benchmark of YHWH’s severity — transmitted through the same oral tradition that carried Israel’s saving history. The urgency of transmission signals that Joel is not describing a natural disaster but a revelation of YHWH’s character and purpose.
4: yeter hagazam ʾakal haʾarbeh wĕyeter haʾarbeh ʾakal hayelek wĕyeter hayelek ʾakal hechasil: “What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust has eaten; what the swarming locust left, the hopping locust has eaten; and what the hopping locust left, the stripping locust has eaten.” This verse is the philological crux of Joel 1. Four Hebrew terms are used: gazam (root: to cut, shear), arbeh (the most common word for locust, from rabah = to be many/swarm), yelek (from a root meaning to lick or hop), and chasil (from chasal = to consume, destroy). The dominant scholarly debate: (1) four different species of locust with slightly different habits; (2) four stages of locust development (egg → larva → nymph → adult); (3) poetic synonyms emphasizing total devastation through accumulation. The progression “what the first left, the second ate” suggests either sequential waves of infestation or a merism: the totality of destruction across all locust varieties and stages. The MKT renders each term distinctively (cutting, swarming, hopping, stripping) to preserve the sense of accumulating destruction without committing to a developmental sequence.
5: hiqitsu shikkorim uvku wehililu kol shote yayin ʿal ʿasis: “Wake up, you drunkards, and weep! Wail, all you wine drinkers, because of the sweet wine — it has been cut off from your lips.” Shikkorim (drunkards, from shakar = to become drunk) are the first audience addressed — those who depend on wine most will feel the loss most immediately. ʿAsis is specifically sweet wine or pressed juice, possibly unfermented grape juice or must; the LXX translates it gleukos (sweet wine/new wine, the same word used in Acts 2:13 when the crowd accuses the Spirit-filled disciples of being full of gleukos). The irony of calling drunkards to weep is deliberate: those who sought pleasure in wine must now mourn its absence.
6: ki goy ʿalah ʿal artsi ʿatsum wĕʾen mispar: “For a nation has invaded my land — powerful and beyond counting.” The locusts are personified as a goy (nation, people) — the same word used for Israel’s enemies. ʿAtsum (powerful, mighty) and ʾen mispar (without number, innumerable) = the classic description of an overwhelming army. Shinnayw kĕshinne ʾaryeh (its teeth like lion’s teeth) and mĕtallĕʿot lavi lo (the fangs of a lioness) apply the two forms of the Hebrew lion — ʾaryeh (lion) and lavi (lioness, the more ferocious) — to describe the locust’s destructive power as predatory rather than merely consumptive.
7: sam gapni lĕshamah utĕʾenati liqĕtsafah: “It has stripped my vine bare and splintered my fig tree.” The vine (gephen) and fig tree (teʾenah) are the emblems of covenantal prosperity (1 Kgs 4:25; Mic 4:4; Zech 3:10). Their destruction is not merely economic but symbolically theological: the covenant blessings are being reversed. Liqĕtsafah (stripped bare, peeled; some read “splintered”) is a strong term — the tree is not merely stripped of fruit but its bark is peeled off, leaving it white and dead. The MKT’s “splintered” emphasizes the totality of destruction.
8: qoni kĕvulat saqqim ʿal baʿal nĕʿureha: “Mourn like a young woman dressed in sackcloth, grieving for the husband of her youth.” Bĕtulah (young woman, virgin) in context here means a young bride whose husband has died; the MKT’s “husband of her youth” follows the MT’s baʿal nĕʿureha (lord/husband of her youth). The mourning gesture — saqqim (sackcloth, rough goat-hair garment worn against the skin as penitential discipline; cf. Gen 37:34; 2 Sam 3:31; Jonah 3:5) — clothes the body in the coarsest material as an expression of extreme grief.
9: nichrat minchah wĕnesek mibbeit YHWH: “The grain offering and the drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD.” Nichrat (is cut off, from karat = to cut) — the same root used for covenant-cutting (karat bĕrit) and for capital punishment. The cessation of the daily minchah (grain offering) and nesek (drink offering) — the tamid, the continuous sacrifice prescribed in Exod 29:38-42 — signals the breakdown of the entire cultic system. Without grain and wine, the temple cult cannot function; YHWH’s house is in mourning with the land.
10: shadad sadeh ʾavĕlah ʾadamah: “The fields are ruined; the soil is in mourning.” Shadad (ruined, devastated) is a violent verb — used for military destruction of cities; the locust plague is linguistically assimilated to an enemy invasion. ʾAvĕlah ʾadamah (the ground mourns) — the land itself is a moral actor, mourning as a person mourns; cf. Isa 24:4; Jer 12:4; Hos 4:3. The three agricultural staples — dagan (grain), tirosh (new wine/fresh grape juice), and yitshar (olive oil) — are the basic triad of Deuteronomic blessing (Deut 7:13; 11:14; 12:17). Their failure = the covenant curse reversed.
11: hobishu ʾikarim helilu korem ʿal chitah wĕʿal sĕʿorah ki ʾavad qetsir sadeh: “Be ashamed, O farmers; wail, O vinedressers — for the wheat and for the barley — because the harvest of the field is lost.” Hobishu (be ashamed, from bosh) = the shame of those who planted expecting a harvest and received devastation instead. The specific mention of chitah (wheat) and sĕʿorah (barley) — the two principal grain crops of ancient Israel — grounds the judgment in agricultural reality. Barley ripened in spring (March-April), wheat in early summer (May-June); the locust plague consuming both means a total agricultural failure.
12: hayĕvĕshah hagephen wĕʾumĕlalah tĕʾenah: “The vine has dried up and the fig tree withers.” The catalogue of perishing trees — rimon (pomegranate), tamar (date palm), tapuach (apple or apricot), “all the trees of the field” — is a comprehensive inventory of orchard agriculture. Hevishah sason min bĕne ʾadam: “joy has withered away from the sons of men” — sason (joy, gladness) personified as something that has itself dried up like a tree. The agricultural devastation produces emotional devastation; the land’s fruitfulness and human flourishing are inseparably linked.
13: chĕgoru wĕsipdu hakkohenim helilu mĕshartei mizbĕach: “Gird yourselves and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar.” Chĕgoru saqqim (gird on sackcloth) is the sacerdotal penitential act: the priests themselves must put on the mourners’ garment. Bou linu bassaqqim (come, spend the night in sackcloth) — the overnight vigil in sackcloth intensifies the mourning; night is when the penitent is most vulnerable and the prayer most desperate (cf. Ps 22:2; Lam 2:19). The priests are called to lead the national mourning in their capacity as intercessors between Israel and YHWH.
14: qaddĕshu tsom qirʾu ʿatsarah ʾisphu zĕqenim kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets beit YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.” Qiddash tsom (consecrate a fast) — fasting was consecrated as a sacred act of corporate humiliation before YHWH (cf. 2 Chr 20:3; Ezra 8:21; Neh 9:1). ʿAtsarah (solemn assembly, from a root meaning to restrain/hold back) = a day of cessation from normal activity for religious assembly; used particularly for the closing assembly of the major festivals (Lev 23:36; Num 29:35). The gathering at “the house of the LORD your God” positions the temple as the center of national repentance.
15: ʾahah layom ki qarov yom YHWH ukĕshod mishshaddai yavoʾ: “Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and it comes as devastation from the Almighty.” Yom YHWH (the Day of the LORD) appears five times in Joel (1:15; 2:1, 11, 31; 3:14) — more than in any other single prophetic book. Mishshaddai (from Shaddai, the Almighty) — the divine epithet El Shaddai is archaic, associated with the patriarchal traditions (Gen 17:1; 28:3). Joel creates a wordplay: kĕshod mishshaddai (as devastation from Shaddai) — shod (destruction, devastation) and Shaddai share consonants, suggesting that the name of the Almighty contains within it the threat of destruction. The LXX renders Shaddai as Pantokrator (All-Ruler), losing the wordplay.
16: halo neghed ʿeinenu ʾokhel nichrat mibbeit ʾeloheinu simchah wagil: “Is not the food already cut off before our eyes, and joy and gladness from the house of our God?” The rhetorical question confirms that the judgment is already underway — it is not future but present reality. Simchah wagil (joy and gladness) from the house of God = the festive worship that should accompany the agricultural harvest celebrations (Deut 16:11, 14: “rejoice before the LORD your God” at the Feast of Weeks and Feast of Booths) — these are silenced along with the grain and wine.
17: ʿavĕshu pĕrudot tachat mĕgrĕphoteihem: “The seeds have shrivelled in the ground.” ʿAvĕshu pĕrudot is one of the most philologically difficult phrases in Joel: ʿavash (to shrivel, wither) appears here uniquely; pĕrudot may mean “seeds” or possibly “grain” (from parad = to separate, hence separated/individual kernels). The LXX and other versions show uncertainty. Mĕgrĕphoteihem (their shovels/clods) = the tools used for planting or the clods of earth. The storehouses (mamĕgurot) are empty and the granaries (ʾasomot) broken down — the entire infrastructure of food storage has collapsed.
18: mah neʾĕnechah bĕhemah navoku ʿadrei vaqar: “How the animals groan! The herds of cattle are confused because there is no pasture for them.” Neʾĕnechah (they groan, from ʾanach = to sigh, groan) — the same root applied to Israel’s groaning under Egyptian slavery (Exod 2:23). The animal world shares in the covenant consequences of human sin; the groaning of animals becomes a kind of wordless prayer (cf. Ps 104:21; Job 38:41). Navoku (are confused, disorientated, from navak = to wander in confusion) — the same root used for Israel in the wilderness and for Pharaoh’s army at the Red Sea (Exod 14:3). The cattle’s confusion mirrors Israel’s own disorientation before YHWH’s judgment.
19: ʾeleikha YHWH ʾeqraʾ ki ʾesh ʾakĕlah neʾot midbar: “To you, O LORD, I cry out. For fire has devoured the open pastures.” The shift from description (vv. 2-18) to direct address (ʾeleikha YHWH ʾeqraʾ) marks the transition to lament prayer — the genre of Psalms 22, 44, 88. ʾEsh ʾakĕlah (fire has devoured) — the “fire” alongside the locusts likely refers to the drought’s scorching heat, which dried up the pastures before the locusts consumed what remained; or possibly literal fire sparked by the drought conditions. The open pastures (neʾot midbar, the wilderness pasturelands) and “all the trees of the field” (ʿets hassadeh) encompass both cultivated and uncultivated land.
20: gam bĕhemot sadeh taʾarog ʾeleikha: “Even the wild animals pant and cry to you, for the water brooks have dried up.” Taʾarog (pants/longs, from ʾarag) = the same verb used in Psalm 42:1 (“as the deer pants for flowing streams, so my soul pants for you, O God”) — the universal longing for YHWH expressed through the animal kingdom’s thirst. The dry ʾaphiqe mayim (water channels/brooks) and the fire consuming the wilderness pastures together describe a total environmental collapse: both water and vegetation have failed. Joel’s ecological vision encompasses the full scope of creation’s suffering when the covenant is broken.
